headline: Washington's passing attack overwhelms Oklahoma in season-opening road test
body: Washington controlled the line of scrimmage from the opening kickoff, and the final stat sheet told the story of a team that simply operated on a different wavelength offensively. The Huskies amassed 367 total yards with Demond Williams Jr. threading passes into soft spots of Oklahoma's secondary all afternoon, finishing 19 of 29 for 265 yards and two touchdowns. Oklahoma's defense, anchored by Schaffer's three tackles and a sack, never quite found its rhythm in coverage, and the Sooners' offensive troubles compounded matters when they surrendered two fumbles to Washington's opportunistic defense.

Glass performed capably for Oklahoma, completing 18 of 27 passes for 144 yards and two touchdowns, but the Sooners' running game mustered only 97 yards on seven carries from Bernard. That inefficiency on the ground left Oklahoma one-dimensional at the worst possible time, forcing Glass into uncomfortable third-and-long situations where Karnley and Washington's pass rush made their presence felt. The disparity in total offense—150 yards—reflected a fundamental gap in execution that proved insurmountable.

Washington's RB Bonner Jr. did his part with 91 rushing yards, but the real catalyst was the ability to generate explosive plays through the air. WR Williams hauled in five catches for 95 yards, and that vertical component kept Oklahoma's safeties honest enough to open running lanes. The Huskies' defense held firm when it mattered, turning Oklahoma's turnover into a decisive advantage in a game where field position and momentum proved everything.
turningPoint: Oklahoma's second fumble in the third quarter, recovered by Washington at midfield with the game still within reach, shifted momentum irreversibly and eliminated any realistic path for the Sooners' comeback.

unsungHero.name: Karnley
unsungHero.why: Four tackles and two sacks established Washington's defensive tone early and forced Glass into hurried decisions, yet Williams Jr.'s passing display overshadowed the foundation Karnley built in the trenches
